TX2: UPDATE accounts SET balance = 900 WHERE id = 1;
     -- BLOCKED until TX1 commits/aborts!
-- Readers block writers вҶ’ significant rendimiento impact
3. Serializable Snapshot Isolation (SSI) вҖў Optimistic: transacciones execute sin blocking вҖў At commit time: check if any conflictos occurred, abort if so вҖў Used by: PostgreSQL SERIALIZABLE (since 9.1) вҖў Performance: much better than 2PL (readers don't block writers) вҖў Trade-off: higher abort rate bajo contention 
-- SSI in PostgreSQL:
BEGIN ISOLATION LEVEL SERIALIZABLE;
  SELECT COUNT(*) FROM doctors WHERE on_call = true AND shift_id = 123;
  -- PostgreSQL tracks that this TX leer desde the doctors tabla
  UPDATE doctors SET on_call = false WHERE id = 'alice' AND shift_id = 123;
COMMIT;
-- If another TX modified filas that THIS TX leer вҶ’ one gets aborted
-- Application MUST reintentar: while (true) { try { doTransaction(); break; } catch (SerializationFailure) { reintentar; } }

sections.heading: GuГӯa prГЎctica de decisiГіn para entrevistas
sections.content:
CuГЎndo utilizarlo each aislamiento level: READ COMMITTED (PostgreSQL predeterminado): вҖў Standard web CRUD applications вҖў Most REST APIs вҖў When leer-modify-escritura is done con atomic operations (UPDATE ... SET x = x + 1) вҖў 90% of aplicaciГіn code lives here REPEATABLE READ / Snapshot Isolation: вҖў Reporting consultas that necesitar a consistente point-in-time view вҖў Backups (consistente instantГЎnea) вҖў Analytics consultas scanning grande datasets вҖў Long-running lote jobs SERIALIZABLE: вҖў Financial transacciones donde correctness > throughput вҖў Booking systems (prevent double-booking) вҖў Any escritura skew scenario the aplicaciГіn puede't handle вҖў Always build reintentar logic cuando using SERIALIZABLE Application-level alternatives to SERIALIZABLE: 
-- 1. Explicit locking (FOR UPDATE)
SELECT * FROM inventory WHERE product_id = 42 FOR UPDATE;
-- Locks this fila, other TXs wait

-- 2. Atomic operations (no leer-modify-escritura cycle)
UPDATE inventory SET quantity = quantity - 1
WHERE product_id = 42 AND quantity > 0;
-- Single statement = atomic, no race condiciГіn

-- 3. UNIQUE constraints (prevent duplicates)
CREATE UNIQUE INDEX idx_booking_unique
  ON bookings (room_id, time_slot);
-- Database enforces uniqueness atomically
REWE delivery sistema ejemplo: вҖў Assigning driver to delivery: use SELECT FOR UPDATE on the driver fila вҖў Confirming delivery: atomic UPDATE con WHERE status = 'IN_TRANSIT' вҖў Generating daily reports: REPEATABLE READ to get consistente instantГЎnea вҖў Financial reconciliation: SERIALIZABLE (if possible) or explicit locking

title: Mapeo de entidades JPA вҖ” Del objeto a la tabla

sections.heading: La incompatibilidad objeto-relacional
sections.content:
Relational databases store datos in tablas con foreign claves. Object-oriented code uses objeto graphs con references. The ORM bridges this gap:

| Relational | OOP |
|-----------|-----|
| Table | Class |
| Row | Instance |
| Column | Field |
| Foreign Key | Object reference |
| JOIN | Navigation (getter) |

JPA is the **specification** (interfaces, annotations). Hibernate is the **implementaciГіn** (real SQL generation, caching, dirty checking).

sections.heading: Transiciones de estado de una entidad
sections.content:
Every JPA entidad exists in one of 4 estados:

```
New (Transient)  вҶ’  persist()  вҶ’  Managed (Persistent)
                                       вҶ“
                                   detach() / close()
                                       вҶ“
                                   Detached
                                       вҶ“
                                   merge()  вҶ’  Managed

Managed  вҶ’  remove()  вҶ’  Removed  вҶ’  flush()  вҶ’  DELETE in DB
```

**Managed** = entidad is tracked by the Persistence Context. Any field change is automГЎticamente detected (dirty checking) and will generate an UPDATE at flush time.

**Detached** = entidad was gestionada but the EntityManager is closed. Changes are NOT tracked. Must call `merge()` to re-attach.

sections.heading: El contexto de persistencia (cachГ© de primer nivel)
sections.content:
The EntityManager maintains a **Persistence Context** вҖ” an in-memoria map of all gestionada entidades.

**Key behaviors:**
1. **GarantГӯa de identidad:** `em.buscar(Post.class, 1)` called twice devuelve the SAME Java objeto
2. **Escritura diferida:** SQL is deferred until flush (transacciГіn commit or explicit flush)
3. **DetecciГіn de cambios:** Hibernate compares real estado vs. loaded estado at flush time
4. **Repeatable leer:** Within the mismo PC, you siempre see the mismo datos (even if DB changed)

```java
Post post = em.buscar(Post.class, 1L); // SELECT вҶ’ entidad cached in PC
post.setTitle("New Title");           // no SQL yet!
em.flush();                           // NOW: UPDATE post SET title='New Title' WHERE id=1
```

**Warning:** The PC grows con cada gestionada entidad. For lote processing, flush+clear periodically to evitar OutOfMemoryError.

sections.heading: Estrategias de generaciГіn de identificadores
sections.content:
| Strategy | How | Batching? | Recommended? |
|----------|-----|:---------:|:------------:|
| `IDENTITY` | DB auto-increment | вқҢ No | Avoid for alto-escritura |
| `SEQUENCE` | DB sequence | вң… Yes | вң… Best choice |
| `TABLE` | Separate tabla | вң… Yes | вқҢ Poor rendimiento |
| `UUID` | Application-generated | вң… Yes | Adecuado para distributed |

**Why IDENTITY kills batching:** Hibernate debe execute INSERT inmediatamente to get the generated ID (necesario for PC identity map). This prevents grouping inserts en a lote.

**Why SEQUENCE is mejor:** Hibernate puede pre-allocate IDs (allocationSize=50) in a single DB call, then assign them in-memoria sin extra roundtrips.

```java
@Id
@GeneratedValue(estrategia = GenerationType.SEQUENCE, generator = "post_seq")
@SequenceGenerator(name = "post_seq", sequenceName = "post_seq", allocationSize = 50)
private Long id;
```

sections.heading: Fundamentos del mapeo

title: Relaciones y estrategias de carga вҖ” El campo minado del rendimiento

sections.heading: Tipos de asociaciones
sections.content:
| Annotation | DB structure | Default Fetch |
|-----------|-------------|:-------------:|
| `@ManyToOne` | FK in child tabla | EAGER вҡ пёҸ |
| `@OneToMany` | FK in child tabla | LAZY вң… |
| `@OneToOne` | Shared PK or FK | EAGER вҡ пёҸ |
| `@ManyToMany` | Join tabla | LAZY вң… |

**Critical:** `@ManyToOne` defaults to EAGER. Always override:
```java
@ManyToOne(fetch = FetchType.LAZY)
@JoinColumn(name = "post_id")
private Post post;
```

Failing to set LAZY on `@ManyToOne` causes unexpected JOINs or secondary SELECTs on cada consulta that devuelve the child entidad.

sections.heading: El dilema de la carga
sections.content:
**Hard-wired carga (entidad annotations) is a trap.** Each use case needs diferente datos:

- List view: Post title only (no comments necesario)
- Detail view: Post + comments (JOIN FETCH)
- Admin view: Post + comments + author profile

**Rule:** Set everything to LAZY in the entidad, then elegir the carga estrategia por consulta:

```java
// вң… Query-time carga вҖ” adapt to the use case
// List view: no fetch
SELECT p FROM Post p WHERE p.status = :status

// Detail view: fetch comments
SELECT p FROM Post p JOIN FETCH p.comments WHERE p.id = :id

// Report: DTO projection (no entidad at all)
SELECT nuevo PostSummaryDTO(p.id, p.title, SIZE(p.comments)) FROM Post p
```

sections.heading: Errores habituales de carga
@BatchSize вҖ” La soluciГіn sencilla para N+1

title: JPQL, Criteria y consultas nativas вҖ” Elegir la herramienta adecuada

sections.heading: JPQL вҖ” SQL orientado a objetos
sections.content:
JPQL operates on **entidades and fields**, not tablas and columnas:

```java
// JPQL uses entidad names and field names
SELECT p FROM Post p WHERE p.status = :status

// vs SQL which uses tabla/columna names
SELECT * FROM post WHERE status = ?
```

**Key features:**
- Named parameters (`:status`) вҖ” prevents SQL injection, reusable
- JOIN FETCH вҖ” solve N+1 at consulta time
- Constructor expressions вҖ” `SELECT nuevo DTO(...)` for projections
- Subqueries, aggregate functions, CASE expressions

**Limitations:** No window functions, no CTEs, no base de datos-specific syntax.

sections.heading: Consultas nativas вҖ” Cuando JPQL no es suficiente
sections.content:
Use native SQL for:
- Window functions (`ROW_NUMBER`, `DENSE_RANK`)
- CTEs (Common Table Expressions)
- Database-specific features (`LATERAL JOIN`, `DISTINCT ON`)
- Complex reporting consultas
- Performance-crГӯtico consultas donde you necesitar full control

```java
@Query(valor = """
    SELECT id, title,
           ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY created_on DESC) as row_num
    FROM post
    WHERE status = :status
    """, nativeQuery = true)
List<Object[]> findRankedPosts(@Param("status") String status);
```

**Warning:** Native consultas bypass the Persistence Context flush. Hibernate's `AUTO` flush mode does NOT flush antes native consultas (unlike JPQL). Call `em.flush()` manually if necesario.

sections.heading: Proyecciones DTO вҖ” La mejor opciГіn para solo lectura
PaginaciГіn вҖ” Offset frente a keyset
sections.content:
**Offset pagination** (standard JPA):
```java
query.setFirstResult(offset);  // OFFSET
query.setMaxResults(limit);    // LIMIT
```
Problem: Database debe escaneo and discard `offset` filas. Page 1000 is much slower than pГЎgina 1.

**Keyset pagination** (no offset вҖ” use last seen clave):
```sql
SELECT * FROM post
WHERE (created_on, id) < (:lastDate, :lastId)
ORDER BY created_on DESC, id DESC
LIMIT 20
```

**Rule of thumb:**
- < 10K total results вҶ’ offset is fine
- > 10K results вҶ’ paginaciГіn por clave
- Infinite scroll UI вҶ’ siempre keyset

JPA doesn't support keyset natively. Use native consulta or a library like jOOQ.

sections.heading: Criteria API вҖ” ConstrucciГіn dinГЎmica de consultas

title: Rendimiento de Hibernate вҖ” N+1, procesamiento por lotes, cachГ© y bloqueos

sections.heading: El problema N+1
Procesamiento por lotes JDBC
sections.content:
Hibernate puede group INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE statements en batches, reducing base de datos roundtrips.

```properties
# Enable batching (recommended: 10-30)
hibernate.jdbc.batch_size=25
hibernate.order_inserts=true
hibernate.order_updates=true
```

**Key rules:**
- `IDENTITY` generators **disable** insert batching (Hibernate needs the ID inmediatamente)
- Use `SEQUENCE` generators con `allocationSize > 1` for lote-friendly inserts
- Batch size 10-30 is the sweet spot вҖ” larger is not linearly better
- Mixing entidad types in the mismo flush breaks batching unless `order_inserts=true`
